item.title: Approach to Venice
item.museum: National Gallery of Art, Washington, D.C.
item.lesson: Nine years later, at the end of his Venice, Turner painted the city from far out on the lagoon — and nearly painted it away. A line of barges crosses the water at sunset; Venice itself is a low band of pearl on the horizon, less a city than a rumor of one. The moon is already up. The sea and sky share one continuous gradient of rose and gold, and the question this exhibition opened with — can light on water be painted? — here gets its most radical answer yet: perhaps only light can be painted, and everything else is a courtesy to the viewer. The critic John Ruskin, Turner's great champion, called this the most perfectly beautiful of all his works. Within a few years detractors were calling the late pictures the daubs of a failing mind. Both camps saw the same thing: a painter who had stopped describing the world and started describing seeing. Tomorrow, night falls — and a painter goes further still.
item.lookFor: Try to find the exact place where water ends and sky begins. You cannot — and that seam, or its absence, is the entire late Turner project in one detail.
